items.correct: Volume of air inhaled or exhaled in one normal breath at rest.
items.misconception: The maximum air your lungs can hold.
items.random_wrong: Air left in the lungs after maximum exhalation.
items.why: Students confuse tidal volume (resting breath, ~0.5L) with vital capacity (max breath, ~4-5L). Different lung volumes serve different purposes - tidal is the normal small ebb and flow.

items.q: Which type of muscle is under voluntary control?
items.why: Students think all muscles can be controlled if we try hard enough. Cardiac and smooth muscle are involuntary - you can't consciously beat your heart. Only skeletal muscle responds to conscious commands.

items.q: According to the inverted-U hypothesis, when is performance best?
items.correct: At an optimal moderate level of arousal.
items.why: Students assume 'more psyched up = better'. The inverted-U shows performance rises with arousal up to an optimum, then DECLINES with excess arousal (the athlete chokes). The optimum varies by skill.

items.q: Main energy fuel for sport is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Carbohydrate. “Vitamins” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: Growth and repair. “Sweating” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many main food groups were listed (carb, prot, fat, vitamin, mineral, fibre)?
items.why: The real figure is 6. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
